source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 15.3.3 SetGID (pp. 293-295)

questions.q: What does SetGID (SGID) do on an executable binary?
options: While it runs, the executor temporarily gains the identity of the file's owner GROUP
It changes the file's owner to root
questions.why: On an executable, SGID grants the owner-group identity during execution (e.g. locate runs as group slocate to read mlocate.db).

questions.q: Which leading octal digit represents SGID?
Add the SetGID permission to the executable myprog (symbolic form).
questions.why: chmod g+s sets SGID; numerically the leading digit is 2.

questions.q: What is the effect of SGID on a DIRECTORY?
options: Files created in it take the directory's group as their owner group (not the creator's primary group)
Only root can delete files in it
The directory can no longer be entered
questions.why: SGID on a directory forces group inheritance - handy for team shares so everything belongs to the shared group.

questions.q: In ls -l you see a lowercase 's' in the group execute position (e.g. -rwxr-s--x). What does it mean?
options: SGID is set and functional (the group has x); a capital S would mean the group lacks x
questions.why: Lowercase s = SGID with group x present (working); uppercase S = SGID set but group x missing.

questions.q: Find all SGID files on the system and long-list them.
questions.why: -perm -2000 matches the SGID bit; worth auditing like SUID.
